danger_signals.visual_form: Collision with visible enemy sprites (Koopas, Goombas, etc.)
danger_signals.meaning: Loss of life or power-down state

danger_signals.visual_form: Red/orange animated liquid graphics in level environment
Screen boundary visual limit, bottomless pit below platform
Moving solid blocks (Smashers) with visual animation
Timer display in HUD counting down, changes color/urgency as time runs low
Large distinct enemy sprite in fortress/castle level, different from standard enemies
danger_signals.meaning: Mandatory combat encounter required to progress

safety_signals.visual_form: Super Mushroom, Fire Flower, Cape Feather pickup items with distinct sprites
safety_signals.meaning: Temporary increased capacity to survive threats or gain new movement abilities

safety_signals.visual_form: Yoshi dinosaur sprite appearing in level or egg sprite
safety_signals.meaning: Extra hit protection; enemy absorption capability

safety_signals.visual_form: Castle/Fortress/Ghost House completion visual (level clear screen)
Distinctive flag/post sprite at far right of level
Circular yellow coin sprites scattered in level
safety_signals.meaning: Resource accumulation; 100 coins grants extra life

faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Playable character position; HUD displays Mario's item box and power state
Alternative player character; takes turns in multiplayer mode

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: King Koopa crown; large spiked shell
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Boss battle sprite in final castle; larger and more dangerous than standard Koopa enemies
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Final world castle; throne room architecture; lava surroundings in Bowser's Castle

faction_indicators.color_identity: Varied colored shells (purple, red, yellow, white, orange, blue, green)
faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Individual crown per Koopaling; unique shell coloring scheme
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Fortress boss sprites; each world's fortress controlled by one named Koopaling
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Fortress environments; each world's fortress entrance marked on overworld map

faction_indicators.color_identity: Pink (Princess Peach); varied dinosaur colors (Yoshi in green, red, yellow variants)
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Castle presence on overworld; Yoshi eggs and mounts in levels

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Winged variants (Koopa Paratroopas); spiked variants; color-coded behavior differences
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Scattered throughout action panels and courses; density increases in dangerous areas

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Smaller Mario sprite; no cape or power-up equipped; item box empty or showing stored power-up
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: One hit from enemy causes immediate life loss; cannot perform certain moves requiring power-ups

world_state_to_visual.world_state: Mario powered-up (Super Mario or Cape Mario state)
world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Larger Mario sprite; cape equipped for Cape Feather state; visible power-up in HUD item box
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Can survive one additional hit; can fly/glide with cape; can break certain blocks with spin jump

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Rapid sprite flashing/blinking animation; star visual effect overlay
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Temporary immunity from enemy contact damage; can defeat enemies on contact

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Yoshi sprite visible underneath Mario; color-coded Yoshi variants (green, red, yellow)
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Extra hit protection (Yoshi takes hit instead); Yoshi can eat enemies and spit as projectiles; enhanced jumping capability

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Timer display in HUD visually changes urgency cues; flashing or color shift as seconds deplete
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Time-pressure gameplay; forced level restart if reaches zero before level completion

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Overworld map shows branching paths unlocking; new world castle/fortress icons appear as paths open
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: New level sets become accessible; escalating difficulty visual design in subsequent worlds

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Fortress or castle interior visual theme; large boss sprite presence; arena-style level layout
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Boss executes scripted attack patterns; cannot progress without boss defeat; boss health may be indicated visually

environmental_storytelling.example: Paths on the overworld connect sequentially to worlds 1-7, with branching optional routes. Fortresses and Ghost Houses mark mini-boss/challenge locations. This visual progression system communicates narrative flow and world geography.

Each world features distinct visual themes (grassland, forest, cave, etc.). Fortresses have imposing fortress architecture; Ghost Houses have haunted mansion design. Bowser's Castle features lava moats and dark stone, communicating danger and the final antagonist's lair.

Dangerous areas (fortresses, late-world levels) show higher enemy sprite density and more varied enemy types. Safe areas and early levels have sparse enemy placement. Visual enemy clustering signals difficulty escalation.

Lava appears as animated red/orange liquid in specific fortress/volcanic levels. Spikes, crushers, and bottomless pits are clearly rendered graphically. Visual hazard design communicates threat without requiring text explanation.

Cape Feathers and Super Mushrooms appear visually in levels before dangerous sections, suggesting those items are needed. Their placement is visually telegraphed to the player through sprite positioning.
Koopalings and Bowser are rendered with larger sprites, unique coloring, and crown imagery distinct from standard enemies. Visual scale and design communicate their special antagonist status.

entity_behavioral_tells.state: Standing on ground with full P-meter
entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Can sprint at maximum speed; sprite shows ready stance
Sprite displays cape; can fly by holding jump; floats/glides through air
Reduced sprite size; single hit causes death; cannot break blocks or perform spin jumps
Follows Mario's movement; sprite stays below Mario; accepts input to eat enemies or spit projectiles
Walks back and forth in predictable pattern; turtle shell sprite animated with walking cycle
Shell slides rapidly across level; can damage other enemies on contact; hazardous to player
Sprite visible with wings attached; follows bouncing/flying patrol patterns; more challenging to avoid than grounded variant
Brown mushroom sprite walks in straight line; simple movement pattern; defeated by jump
Large distinct sprite; executes attack patterns; takes multiple hits; visible in fortress interior
Massive sprite; lava arena environment; complex attack pattern; final antagonist encounter requiring defeat to complete game

notes: Super Mario World communicates danger primarily through visual enemy design, environmental hazards (lava, spikes, crushers), and sprite-based power states. Faction communication is minimal in traditional political sense but clear through character coloring (Mario red, Luigi green, Bowser dark brown). The game uses visual progression on the overworld map to communicate world-state and narrative flow. Danger escalates visually through increased enemy density in later worlds and fortress environments. Safety is signaled through power-up appearance, item box storage display, and checkpoint/castle completion visuals. The game relies entirely on sprite rendering, color-coding, and spatial layout to communicate meaning—no text-based UI except score/time/lives display.
